data.title: CTO Realty Growth: Buy A High-Quality REIT Offering 8.4% Yield
data.description:
Thesis

A Company that deserves a "BUY" recommendation, according to my view, is CTO Realty Growth (NYSE:CTO).

It is a stock more suited to patient income investors who are comfortable with the volatility of retail real estate.

It combines interesting lease dynamics, visible organic growth through signed-not-open leases (SNO, i.e. signed leases that have not yet started generating income) and active capital management with share repurchases and property recycling.

Recent quarterly results are not “explosive” but they build a foundation for improvement in 2026 and 2027.

The increase in core FFO (Funds From Operations, operating profits before depreciation and amortization) in absolute numbers, the advanced re-leasing of large anchor boxes and the SNO pipeline of $ 5.5 million in annual rents create a fairly clear path to higher recurring revenues.

At the same time, leverage remains elevated, with net debt to EBITDA at 6.7x.CTO looks more like a “buy under pressure” opportunity than a yield trap. This depends on whether the management will continue to execute on its SNO, anchors and deleveraging plan.

As I see it, the long term and stable character of the investment is seen on the board as the stock is gaining 52% the last decade.

This return comes from dividends as the stock price remains unchanged.

Leasing Momentum and Portfolio Upgrade

This return comes from dividends as the stock price remains unchanged. On the operational front, the picture is clearly positive.

Since the beginning of the year until the end of December, the company completed 482,000 square feet of total leasing activity, 424,000 square feet of those were comparable leases with an average base rent increase of 21.7%.

For a shopping center portfolio, this indicates a strong negotiating position in individual local markets.

In Q3 143,000 square feet of new leases, renewals and expansions were signed with an average base rent of $23 per square foot.

Of the 125,000 comparable square feet, rents increased by 10.3%.

The company is not filling gaps with discounting but is improving the average rent per square foot. Same-property NOI rose by 2.3%.

The rate is not impressive by itself but it reflects:

• replacement of old tenants with more productive uses, such as in Beaver Creek, where the old movie theater was replaced by a Onelife Fitness gym and

• strong performance of small stores in West Broad Village, Plaza at Rockwall and Ashford Lane.

The Management’s approach is also worth noting: in several cases (particularly at Shops at Legacy) potential tenants with weak financials or excessive demands for tenant improvements were rejected.

This shows that the goal is not “any” occupancy but quality leasing that will support long-term NOI and property value.

SNO Pipeline and Anchor Box Re-Leasing

At the center of the investment case is the re-leasing of the ten major anchor boxes.

Six have already been leased while the remaining four are in advanced negotiations.

So far, the signed contracts correspond to approximately $2.5 million in new annual rents.

The potential total contribution from the ten anchors is $4 to $4.5 million, if management's goals are achieved.

The goal of a positive cash leasing spread of 40% to 60% across these ten anchors is ambitious but not unrealistic especially in markets where the previous leases were tenant-friendly.

If these plans be realized it will be a significant driver of NOI growth itself, not just a gradual improvement in occupancy.

At the same time, the signed-not-open pipeline amounts to $5.5 million in annual rents.

This corresponds to approximately 5.3% of the portfolio's existing cash base rents.

The CFO was quite specific about the timeline:

• approximately 75% of the amount (around $4 million) is expected to be recognized in 2026,

• the integration will be gradual, from approximately $0.5 million in the first quarter to approximately $1.5 million in the fourth,

• by the end of 2026, all tenants currently in the SNO pipeline will have opened and are paying regular cash rent, with a full impact of $5.5 million in 2027.

Shops at Legacy is a clear example of a value-add opportunity.

The former WeWork space has essentially been re-leased into two new uses:

• a 20,000 square foot private members' club,

• a 30,000 square foot coworking space, scheduled to open by the end of 2026.

Along with approximately 60,000 square meters of small stores (catering, gyms, retail) signed in the last two years, the center's occupancy rate has risen to close to 85%.

The remaining vacancy is mainly smaller surfaces where the company states that it is consciously selective.

The Company is also planning to buy a new commercial center in South Florida.

Balance Sheet, Capital Allocation and Share Repurchases

The Company took advantage of the interest rate stabilization window to improve its debt profile.

Before the end of the quarter, two debt transactions totaling $150 million were completed:

• a new five-year $125 million loan maturing in September 2030,

• a $25 million increase in an existing loan maturing in September 2029.

Both loans carry a SOFR interest rate plus a margin depending on leverage.

Thanks to interest rate swap agreements already in place, the effective interest rate starts at around 4.2%.

It is also expected to adjust to around 4.7% from 2026, based on the current balance sheet structure.

The proceeds were used to repay a $65 million loan maturing in 2026 and to reduce the balance on the credit line.

After these moves, the Company has approximately $170 million in total liquidity ($161 million available in the revolving credit facility and $9 million in cash).

It is noted that only $17.8 million of loans mature in 2026, which significantly reduces short-term financial risk.

The net debt to EBITDA ratio is at 6.7x, slightly improved from 6.9x in the previous quarter.

The most important is that only the integration of SNO leases is estimated to be able to remove part of the leverage.

This does not take into account possible sales of mature properties (asset recycling).

On the shareholder side, management is demonstrating in practice that it considers the valuation attractive:

• repurchases of $9.3 million at an average price of $16.27 per share,

• activation of a new $10 million repurchase program after the completion of the previous one.

The CEO even called current price levels “almost ridiculous” with a P/FFO of under 9x and a dividend yield of close to 10%.

The company also has the ability to recycle capital by selling stabilized assets at lower cap rates and redeploying proceeds into higher-yield opportunities or share repurchases.

As i see it this capital allocation strategy supports the long-term investment case.

Risks

CTO Realty Growth has its own dangers despite its positive signs.

First of all leverage is high. Manageable but high.

Net debt to EBITDA is around 6.7x, which does not leave much room if things go off plan.

Especially if there are delays in new leases opening or in filling the 40,000-square-foot anchor at Carolina Pavilion would make it harder to reduce debt quickly.

If the demand in specific local markets slows down then the reduction of debt will take longer time.

Second, the business model is clearly exposed to retail risk.

The assets are mainly open-air malls with more “durable” uses (supermarkets, fitness, catering, services).

Therefore, the sector is sensitive to consumption slowdown or to changes to the preferences of the tenants.

Additionally, the dependence on large anchors means that a potential departure of an important tenant could affect negatively the center's profitability.

Third, the value-add strategy requires significant expenditures for tenant improvements and leasing commissions.

As already mentioned, space development subsidies were very low in the first half of the year and increased in the third quarter.

They are expected to remain elevated in the Q4 as new anchor leases are delivered.

If these costs exceed initial estimates or if tenants do not perform as expected, the return on invested capital will decline.

Fourth, the investment case is largely based on the success of the SNO pipeline and the upgrade of the ten anchors.

This is essentially a concentrated set of movements.

Any slippage in the schedule, problems with a specific tenant (e.g. in the co-working or the private members club) or turbulence in local markets could turn the current “visible growth” into a more uncertain scenario.

Finally, valuation risk should not be underestimated.

The risk relates to the possibility that the market will decide to permanently price retail REITs at lower multiples. And this is critical regardless of FFO improvement.

And so, the stock may remain “cheap” for longer than an impatient investor would like.

Conclusion

CTO Realty Growth is in an in-between phase: it is neither a “deep value” story in distress nor a “growth” REIT with impressive growth rates.

It is, more, a case of stable commercial real estate with a specific plan to increase revenues the next two years.

The plan is based on four points:

• strong performance in new leases and positive rent increases on comparable leases,

• a substantial SNO pipeline of $ 5.5 million expected to feed into 2026 and 2027,

• restructuring of ten large anchor boxes with the aim of significantly increasing rents,

• disciplined balance sheet management and aggressive share repurchases at prices that management itself considers particularly low.

For an investor looking for a combination of high dividend yield and visible, though gradual, FFO growth over a 2–3 year horizon, CTO could be an attractive position.

The key is being comfortable with retail exposure and higher leverage. If the company achieves its goals in leasing and deleveraging, earnings and valuation could improve.

As far as I am concerned the stock is an undervalued opportunity instead of a value trap and is suitable for those that can wait and are not affected by the negative sentiment in the retail real estate.

data.title: Upstart: Improving Fundamentals And A Still Discounted Price
data.description:
Introduction:

Upstart (UPST) has been able to continue executing its vision of using AI to help automate consumer lending and the most recent quarterly results showed significant progress across the board.

The business has expanded its ability to underwrite loans and verify potential borrowers while also securing long term funding commitments from its partners which should stabilize the business even if economic conditions deteriorate over the coming years.

Given the stock's decline over the past year, shares are trading at compelling forward multiples and therefore I am rating Upstart a BUY.

Business Description & Key Considerations:

Upstart is a company that helps reduce the cost and complexity of borrowing money through the use of advanced AI systems. The firm's models have been trained over 98 million borrower repayment events, and they are reportedly excellent at considering the risk of lending to individual consumers while taking into account macroeconomic factors as well.

Upstart's platform currently supports personal, auto, and home equity loans, and the firm's long term vision is to offer even more options for users over time. The company generates revenue from both platform fees, where lenders pay Upstart to use its AI lending marketplace, and referral fees where partners are charged for each loan originated through the platform.

Throughout FY25, 91% of the loans originated through Upstart were entirely automated, and around 87% of these loans were funded by institutional investors and Upstart's bank lending partners. Also, around 50% of funding currently comes from committed arrangements with investors where they specify the amount of risk they're willing to take on along with the returns they expect.

Securing commitments is incredibly important as they provide stability when credit markets tighten since Upstart's partners are contractually obligated to purchase loans that meet the agreed upon criteria. It should be noted that the number of loans completed entirely using AI automation has stagnated compared to the year ago period, which indicates to me that the firm's internal models may have reached a point where achieving further improvements will be difficult and take some time to materialize.

While Upstart's business is focused on underwriting loans, the company also uses its AI models for things like income and identity verification, and to detect if potential borrowers have already taken out multiple loans from many different lenders simultaneously.

Upstart has been able to substantially improve the speed of its personal loan pricing engine recently, which allows the firm to now run larger and more complex underwriting models that have a higher number of parameters and data inputs.

Similarly, Upstart has created a new machine learning model to ensure its take rates remain in the "goldilocks zone" so to speak (not too high, and not too low). For certain borrowers, Upstart can raise take rates without hurting conversions, and in other instances they must lower fees in order to remain competitive.

Furthermore, the company has introduced a new method related to marketing, which has worked so far and led to a 50% increase in originations at the same level of marketing expenditures.

While Upstart did achieve a number of improvements across its tech stack, its AI models likely misinterpreted some macroeconomic indicators during the quarter which led to the company reducing the number of loan approvals it granted and increasing interest rates for the borrowers it did approve.

This may've left a sour taste in many users' mouths as numerous individuals with solid credit were likely unable to secure the financing they desired through the platform. While the models have since corrected themselves (at least according to management), this event underscores my view that the largest risk to Upstart's business is reputational in nature.

If this continues to happen on a routine basis, people will simply stop using the platform to get loans and will go to one of the many alternative credit sources that exist instead.

Shifting gears, Upstart has made significant progress in streamlining the application process for consumers who wish to open a home equity line of credit (HELOC), as automatic approvals have grown from 1% back in June of this year to 20% by the end of October.

If this metric continues on this trajectory, the company will likely see an uptick in the number of consumers applying for HELOCs through the platform given the ease and simplicity of taking out a loan through the platform relative to many other traditional mortgage lenders.

Finally, right after the release of the most recent quarterly earnings results, Upstart inked a one year forward-flow agreement with Castlelake. Under this deal, the alternative investment firm is set to buy up to $1.5 billion worth of consumer loans that will be originated through Upstart's platform.

While this does introduce a degree of customer concentration risk for the company given the size of the deal, the two have successfully worked together numerous times in the past and there is nothing to indicate that this agreement will work out any differently.

As a matter of fact, I believe that this announcement should act as a strong tailwind as it shows that Upstart has been able to consistently deliver on its promises to this specific group of sophisticated investors to help them identify strong borrower candidates and earn reasonable rates of return.

Financials & Valuation:

Upstart reported relatively strong third quarter results, with transaction volume in dollars rising 80% YoY to ~$2.85 billion, whereas the number of loans approved during the quarter increased 128% YoY to 428K. The significantly faster growth rate in the number of loans approved reflects lower loan sizes as the firm's AI models uncovered many low risk borrowers who applied for small loans.

Upstart's conversion rate, which effectively measures how many borrowers that signaled interest in taking out a loan through the platform ended up getting funded, increased 430bps YoY to 20.6%. However, management noted that the company's models actually reduced the number of approvals sequentially due to higher macroeconomic risk.

Upstart's total revenue increased 70.9% YoY to $277.1 million in Q3 FY25, with net fee revenue growing 54.3% YoY to $258.5 million. Drilling deeper, platform and referral fees increased by around 61.6% YoY to $216.9 million, and servicing fees rose 24.8% YoY to $41.7 million.

Net interest income (which includes adjustments to the fair value of loan securities held on Upstart's balance sheet) came in at $18.6 million, which is a sharp turnaround compared to the $5.5 million loss incurred during the year ago period.

While there were a lot of positives this past quarter, the firm's variable expenses tied to its loan origination business (including borrower acquisition, verification, and servicing costs) increased 70.4% YoY to $111.1 million. This led to the company's contribution margin declining considerably from 61% a year ago down to just 57%.

Finally, Upstart's total operating expenses increased 22.3% YoY to $253.4 million, demonstrating substantial operating leverage. This allowed the firm to post a positive operating income balance, coming in at $23.7 million compared to a $45.2 million loss in Q3 FY24. Similarly, Upstart generated a net profit of nearly $32 million this most recent quarter, compared to a $6.7 million loss last year.

Having gone over the financial results, let's now take a look at Upstart's current valuation. The stock trades at a modest 18.7x FY26 earnings, which is largely a result of the sell-off over the past year (with shares declining by around 34% over that time frame).

I personally believe that this presents a buying opportunity, as the company is expected to benefit from both high revenue growth and operating leverage over the next two years (with EPS expected to expand more rapidly than the top line).

Furthermore, Upstart's vision to begin offering more borrowing options to users should increase engagement on the platform and boost both transaction volume and the total number of loan approvals.

Conclusion:

Upstart's third quarter print demonstrated tangible momentum in terms of originations and operating profitability, which is a major positive in my view. The company's ability to continue iterating and improving its AI models, while also securing new partnerships and helping its borrower base signal immense operational strength.

While the firm's reputational risk and sensitivity to the macro economy should remain top of mind, I believe that the fundamentals of the business will keep improving and the relatively low valuation multiples justify a BUY rating on the stock.

data.title: VIOV: Continued Underperformance Is Likely
data.description:
The Vanguard S&P Small-Cap 600 Value Index Fund ETF Shares (VIOV), launched on 09/07/2010 and managed by The Vanguard Group, Inc., provides exposure to the small-cap segment of the equity market filtered based on value characteristics.

In this article, I am going to go over the portfolio, the recent performance of this ETF, and the macro conditions that shape the outlook for it. Although this fund is well-diversified and the value factor makes it defensive in times of heightened uncertainty like this, there are implications that point to continued underperformance in at least the near term.

Portfolio and Performance

The portfolio is as diversified as you'd expect from a small-cap ETF, with the most meaningful exposure being represented by Sandisk (SNDK), a spinoff company that experienced its stock price increase by more than 500% in 2025. Aside from that, the weights appear to be very conservative, with the rest of the top holdings accounting for 0.8-1% of the portfolio each.

The sector distribution is equally modest, with tech stocks accounting for only ~17% of the portfolio, a far cry from the current exposure of the large-cap segment. As value stocks are most often found in bank stocks using traditional valuation metrics as criteria, financial stocks make up the majority of the portfolio at ~19%.

Still, consumer cyclical stocks are a meaningful part of the ETF and make VIOV highly sensitive to consumer sentiment and, therefore, the macro backdrop. Speaking of which, from the YTD performance relative to the S&P 500, it's evident that the market felt even less comfortable with small caps (even value ones) back in April. YTD, VIOV has underperformed the broader market by a meaningful margin.

This trend is unlikely to change based on what we know about the macroeconomic conditions.

Opportunities and Risks

Inflation Expectations

Recent inflation readings, such as CPI, put inflation around 3%. Core CPI is now at the same level, hinting at a structural reflation trend.

Consumers expect inflation to keep rising, and that is fairly consistent with the current price pressures implied by higher tariffs. With the most likely scenario being that companies will keep exhausting their pre-tariff inventories, we might actually see more than a 3.2% rate in the near term; Gregory Daco from EY-Parthenon expects that tariffs are going to result in a 0.8 percentage point increase by early 2026.

With small caps being more sensitive to inflationary pressures than large caps, the implication is that VIOV constituents will feel the impact of raw-material costs more immediately. This translates into margin pressures, as the recent lag between tariff implementation and the price increases indicates both front-loading and cost absorption due to uncertainty about the consumer's appetite.

Labor Market Conditions

The labor market, on the other hand, acts as a brake for inflation growth to some extent because wage inflation pressures are supposed to be modest, given the rapid job growth deceleration the economy experienced recently. Nonfarm payroll growth has fallen from well above 1% early this year to just 0.83% lately.

Additionally, unemployment has risen to 4.4%, according to the September jobs report, and the number of claims for unemployment insurance remaining high implies further increases ahead.

Actually, Global Investment Research estimates unemployment to be as high as 4.5% by the end of the year, which doesn't seem so far-fetched anymore.

Because many of VIOV's holdings don't have a lot of flexibility when it comes to labor costs, which are usually significant, a softening labor market can be seen as a relative advantage for this ETF. On the other hand, rising unemployment implies weaker spending, which is a risk for VIOV. Currently, the labor market risks outweigh the opportunities. Though inflation is not expected to rise aggressively, the same level of confidence is not often exhibited by analysts when it comes to the labor market.

Monetary Policy Path

Some offsetting is provided by the monetary policy path, however, which is probably going to lean towards accelerated easing and not the same pace of rate cuts we have seen up to this point. That's because the labor market represents a greater risk than inflation, as we've seen above.

The Fed lowered the federal funds rate by 25 bps in the last meeting, and the overwhelming majority of bets imply that the market largely expects it to cut by another 25 bps in nine days. This is consistent with the current balance of risks and the reasoning that amid a government shutdown and delayed reports, the safest thing to do is to keep easing.

With lower borrowing costs, small caps are expected to benefit directly since they have much less financing cost flexibility than large caps. At the same time, lower rates will translate into higher valuation multiples.

Growth Trends

With the US economy entering a softer growth phase, the main risk for small caps is related to an opportunity cost. Real GDP growth averaged 1.4% in the first half of this year, and economic forecasts point to a rate that is below historical averages for the next year too.

Value is a defensive factor, but in a scenario where the economy slows down, the cyclical nature of small caps will prove a meaningful headwind for VIOV. Value is outweighed by the small-cap factor and is unlikely to help the ETF to perform equally or better than mid-cap and large-cap peers. The small-cap factor is aggressive and does better when the pace of growth picks up.

Conclusion

Therefore, I am rating VIOV a hold. I am a big fan of value investing, and small caps are the segment where you can find good opportunities. But this ETF is not actively managed, and the outlook implies further underperformance for it.

Now, I am always open to challenging my views because being an ideologue in investing and trading can prove expensive. So if you have a different opinion, this platform is the right place to discuss both strategy and the various options with which we can position our portfolios. Go ahead and leave a comment below to let me know what you think.

Otherwise, please let me know if you found this article useful. It means a lot!

Thank you for reading.

data.title: The Predictability Premium: Why Clearway Energy Wins
data.description:
Elevator Thesis

Predictability and growth are the two attributes that are surprisingly rare in the Renewable Electricity industry. Interestingly, Clearway Energy, Inc. (CWEN) (CWEN.A) is delivering this two-punch combo.

Clearway purchases long-term clean-energy assets yielding reliable cash instead of pursuing large, risky development bets.

This proves to be a smart strategy when we look at the company’s recent outcomes. For perspective, the cash flow is increasing, operations are picking up, and new projects are coming in at a normal pace.

Surely the debt is high, but the broader picture is heading in the right direction. Clearway is acquiring assets that actually pay back, and this instills confidence for the coming years.

Furthermore, the company has a strong dividend and its valuation remains reasonable in comparison to its peers.

Spoiler alert: Clearway is a buy right now!

Clearway Q3 '25 Earnings

Clearway’s Q3 was not perfect, but the key trends are encouraging. The net income increased to $60 million as compared to $27 million last year. Lower taxes were helpful, but the actual stimulus was operating efficiency. Adjusted EBITDA increased to $385 million from $354 million, even as revenue declined by 11.7% to $429 million. This means that Clearway is working to convert current assets into cash instead of pursuing top-line growth.

Moreover, CAFD also rose to $166 million compared to $146 million a year ago, despite the higher-rate environment. This points to disciplined operations and strong asset performance. Flexible Generation availability increased to 92.5% from 87.5% due to low outages. Meanwhile, Renewables and Storage output increased to 5,151 thousand MWh compared to 4,955 thousand MWh as new facilities became available and wind conditions improved.

These findings made management comfortable to reduce the 2025 CAFD guidance to $420-$440 million, indicating that the company is stabilizing while still growing.

That said, this growth push is draining the balance sheet. The liquidity has decreased to $834 million versus $1.33 billion at year’s end. Whereas, cash (TTM) is around $251 million versus total debt of $9.2 billion. Hence, the cash/debt ratio is extremely low and the net leverage remains high.

Growth That Pays Off

Clearway is quietly carving a noticeable path for growth. For example, the management emphasized more than 2 GW of opportunities for 2026-2027, with a solid and concrete pipeline.

Additionally, Clearway relies on repowerings and drop-downs in order to grow and remain flexible. Projects like San Juan Mesa prolong the asset life and enhance their production, which directly enhances CAFD, particularly in wind-heavy areas.

Furthermore, the 613 MW of solar acquisition in October will add additional predictable cash flow across eight states with approximately ten-year contracts. Clearway’s net investment of $210-230 million is undoubtedly big, however, risk remains in check since the portfolio is completely contracted.

Altogether, these moves make the company's growth evident and low-risk. The money from new projects will sustain the increasing financial needs. In the meantime, management’s 2030 targets give a clear long-term direction and a reason to stay confident.

Now, the question revolves around leverage. Will cash flow from new projects settle increasing debt costs? The answer depends on disciplined capital placement and more emphasis on investments that maximize returns. If Clearway stays focused, such growth initiatives will not only increase the asset base but will also increase CAFD and provide sustainable dividends.

A High-Yield Edge

Clearway’s dividend stands out as a core strength for income investors. The yield is sustainable and represents discipline in capital allocation at $1.81 annually, with a payout ratio of approximately 74%.

Both share classes have a slight difference in forward yields. CWEN.A currently has a 5.35% forward yield compared to 5.01% of CWEN which is a slight discount. This makes CWEN.A a little more appealing to yield-oriented investors despite reduced liquidity.

A five-year dividend growth rate of 13.28% is an indicator of structural resilience. In contrast to businesses that pursue rapid growth, Clearway takes advantage of long-term contracts to grow cash returns gradually. This reliability provides assurance that payouts will continue even amid market volatility.

The differences between the share classes influence the investor’s choice. The liquidity of CWEN (trading over 1 million shares daily) is high enough to cater to institutional investors who require flexibility and large positions. CWEN.A (trading a few hundred thousand shares per day) is light-volume and is more appealing to retail investors or long-term income investors. Both provide the same economic exposure, but with a slightly higher yield, CWEN.A presents an incremental upside for investors who are ready to have lower liquidity in exchange for increased cash flow.

Valuation Snapshot

Clearway's valuation is both promising and cautious.

The stock is appealing on a trailing basis. The GAAP P/E (TTM) is at 14.6x, approximately 29% under the sector median of 20.5x, showing that investors are not overpaying on recent profitability. Its P/CF (TTM) at 5.8x stands better than the sector's 9x, offering a 35.5% discount. The company is generating cash effectively as compared to its historical earnings.

However, forward expectations reflect expected pressure on future earnings. CWEN.A is trading at a forward P/E of 42x, which is over two times the sector's 20.5x, while CWEN clocks in at 34.9x. These numbers fail to reflect the cash strength of the company. Non-cash elements of GAAP earnings have the effect of lowering reported earnings, even though cash flow and dividends are strong. This will help the company to sustain payouts and cope with short-term challenges.

Peer Comparison: I chose NextEra Energy( NEE) and Brookfield Renewable( BEP)( BEPC) to do a comparative analysis since these are the largest U.S. yieldcos and offer a benchmark for valuation and performance.

CWEN’s GAAP P/E is significantly lower than NEE’s high-20s range and far below Brookfield's 96x, whose low earnings base overstates the multiple. The PEG ratio of about 0.12x further proves that the stock appears cheap compared to projected growth. Clearway is evidently providing the best value on a pure equity basis.

Furthermore, enterprise metrics offer more insight. Forward EV/Sales at 12x is slightly higher than NextEra but lower than Brookfield. Indeed, the market is paying a modest premium for revenue. However, EV/EBITDA of 13.8x is less than that of NextEra's 16.4x and much less than Brookfield's 28.7x, indicating efficient operating profitability.

All in all, Clearway provides reasonable valuation, high cash generation, and stable income.

Fair Value Range

Currently, Clearway trades around $34, up about 23% YOY.

I valued Clearway using a DCF model. The model begins with the 2024 FCF of approximately $483 million and projects it forward over the next five years with varying growth projections. Each scenario implies a discount rate that matches the risk profile. I then subtract approximately $8.9 billion of net debt after computing the present value of these cash flows and the terminal value.

Bear case: Considering that the projects would progress slowly, free cash flow only grows at 1% a year, and the WACC is 7 - 7.5%. This provides a value of $26-$27 per share, or around 21% lower than $34. Most cash flows are contracted, so the downside is limited.

Base case: It assumes constant project execution, with 3% annual free cash flow growth, at a WACC of 6%. The model provides $47-$48 per share or about 40% above $34. It's a balance of pipeline growth, contracted cash flow, and reasonable financing costs; therefore, it’s the most likely scenario.

Bull Case: It uses a higher free cash flow growth of 5% a year, a WACC between 4.5 and 5%, and smooth execution. The value amounts to $71-$73 per share, which is more than double the current price.

A probability-weighted approach assuming 25% bear, 60% base, and 15% bull yields a fair value of around $46-48 per share.

At $34, Clearway trades below base-case and probability-weighted values. Project timelines, refinancing, and merchant exposure should be monitored by long-term investors who may be looking at good upside. Staggered purchasing can help to reduce risk.

Risks

To begin with, Clearway has a sizable ratio of variable-rate borrowings in its debt profile, implying that rising rates hit almost immediately. Each increase in the cost of financing consumes profits and CAFD, and unlike peers with a fixed rate structure, Clearway is less insulated. Such sensitivity can cause unstable near-term earnings.

Secondly, renewable electricity is not a “set it and forget it” business. Repowerings, maintenance, and grid upgrades are costly. Assuming that the costs remain high while Clearway proceeds with repowerings and the new 613 MW solar portfolio, the cash flows would temporarily be constrained, particularly with the already high leverage.

Finally, the strategy of Clearway is heavily based on repowering timelines, contract renewals, and the flawless integration of the drop-downs. If any of these slip, either due to permitting delays, supply chain problems, or less than expected asset output, the planned CAFD uplift can be reduced rapidly.

Moreover, since the business operates under long-term contracts, any snag with utility counterparties or changes in state policy would decrease the visibility that investors value.

Takeaway

Clearway deserves your attention.

Yes, debt is considerable, and earnings could be under pressure in the short term, but the company operates a disciplined, predictable model. It purchases cash-generating assets, carries out smart repowerings, and maintains growth without taking risky bets. Dividends are good, cash flow is getting better, and valuation remains quite reasonable relative to peers. Clearway has the potential to provide stable returns to income-oriented investors in the long run. The strategy is simple but effective. Over time, such a consistent approach is likely to be profitable. If you look beyond temporary caveats, Clearway is a reliable option.

data.title: Suncor Energy: A Value Machine With Rising Efficiency
data.description:
Suncor Energy (SU) (SU:CA), a Calgary-based integrated oilsands giant, offers a safe way to play oil’s potential upside. With robust cash flows, the company keeps returning value to investors at full speed through dividends and buybacks.

Efficiency has significantly improved since the appointment of Richard Kruger as CEO in early 2023, but the progress has not been fully priced in, and the stock still lags peers on valuation metrics.

I initiate coverage of Suncor with a ‘buy’ and a price target of $60, based on a more reasonable EV/EBITDA multiple than the market currently reflects. My target implies a potential upside of about 30% in addition to dividend income.

Quick Overview

Suncor was created in 1979 when Sun Oil, which dated back to 1917, merged its conventional oil businesses with Great Canadian Oil Sands. It has since grown into Canada’s second-biggest oilsands developer by market cap.

Upstream, the company produces more than 0.8 million barrels a day, with key assets located near the Athabasca River in Alberta. Downstream, it refines nearly half a million barrels a day in Edmonton, Montreal, Sarnia, and Commerce City, Colorado. Suncor also runs the Petro-Canada retail and wholesale distribution network.

Making Progress

Oil prices have largely declined since 2023, but Suncor’s operating cash flows have held up and formed an overall uptrend, as shown in the chart below. This is attributable to improving efficiency under the leadership of Kruger, an energy veteran and former CEO of Imperial Oil. He pushed ahead with measures to simplify the corporate structure, lay off redundant workers, cut debts, and focus on key operating metrics.

Over the last two years, the company has met or beaten its operational targets on a fairly consistent basis, including asset utilization, financial leverage, breakeven oil price, and refinery turnaround time.

In early 2024, Suncor made a three-year plan with two key goals: boost free funds flow (normalized at $75 WTI) by C$3.3 billion from about C$5 billion and reduce WTI breakeven price by $10 from $53. By the end of 2024, the company had already accomplished 70% of both tasks, and it’s well on track to meet the full targets by 2026.

Shareholder Returns

Suncor is committed to returning all its free cash flows to investors through dividends and buybacks. Since 2022, the company has distributed C$10.2 billion of dividends and repurchased C$12.5 billion of shares, according to quarterly filings through September. The combined value accounts for about 30% of Suncor’s current market cap. In the 12 months through September, dividends and buybacks added up to C$6 billion, or about 8% of the market cap.

We want to be a cash machine that invests wisely and returns capital to shareholders predictably and reliably. It's taken us a couple of years to get all the bells and whistles in place to improve fundamental performance. (CEO Kruger, Nov. 5 earnings call)

In terms of the dividend to the investors, we view that as a commitment and a promise… The cash return strategy, though, of buybacks, in our view, it is one of the best mechanisms to get cash back to our shareholders and really drive the per-share value… (Kris Smith, Executive Advisory, Nov. 5 earnings call)

A modest financial leverage helps support the company’s dividends and buybacks. Net debt currently accounts for 55% of adjusted funds from operations for the trailing 12 months, down from about 70% in 2023. The ratio of net debt to shareholders’ equity has dropped to 16% from about 23% in 2023.

Oilsands Assets

The development of oilsands usually requires a much larger upfront capital outlay than conventional oil production. However, after the initial investment, oilsands assets often enjoy the huge advantage of low natural decline and long reserve lives.

Suncor’s oilsands portfolio consists of large mines and in-situ facilities in northern Alberta with a 24-year remaining life, based on about 7 billion barrels of proved and probable reserves. The overall decline rate is minimal, with zero decline for the Fort Hills and Syncrude mines and a 10% rate for some in-situ operations.

Canada’s oilsands developers, along with US firms in the Permian Basin, have become the lowest-cost oil producers in North America, according to a report by Enverus Intelligence Research. The appeal of oilsands rises as the cost of US shale production climbs amid waning inventories. The average WTI breakeven price for new shale wells has risen to $70 and may increase further to $95 over the next 10 years, Enverus estimates. By comparison, Suncor’s breakeven has dropped below $46, with more downside projected by the company.

Facing macro weakness and US tariffs, Canada plans to spend more than C$100 billion on infrastructure over the next five years. The stimulus program, also known as ‘build, baby, build,’ is expected to include oil pipelines and transport networks that may provide a boost to oilsands developers.

Valuation

Suncor has an enterprise value of $62.5 billion as of Nov. 28, equivalent to 5.6 times estimated 2025 EBITDA, according to Seeking Alpha data. The multiple falls shy of an already cheap median of 6.4 times for the oil and gas sector in North America. As shown in the chart below, the stock looks the cheapest among big oilsands developers in Canada and lags behind some integrated oil majors in America.

With quality assets, robust shareholder returns, and rising efficiency, Suncor deserves a more reasonable valuation, at least on par with major oilsands peers in Canada. I value the stock with a forward EV/EBITDA of 7.2 times, based on the average multiple between Imperial Oil, Canadian Natural Resources, and Cenovus Energy. This ratio, combined with an estimated 11.2 billion EBITDA, $10.4 billion debt, and $2.1 billion cash, implies a fair market cap of $72.2 billion and a price target of $60 per share.

Key Risks

I’m cautiously optimistic about oil prices over the next 12 months due to signs of tight supply and AI-driven demand for energy. However, if WTI drops significantly from current levels, Suncor will probably face a margin squeeze and lower free funds flow, which in turn will weaken shareholder returns.

The US market accounted for about 15% of Suncor’s revenue in 2024. Currently, most energy products from Canada qualify for tariff exemption under the United States-Mexico-Canada Agreement, but any change to the exemption or new tariffs against Canadian oil may still affect the company’s sales.

Conclusion

Suncor has strong cash flows and commits to returning all its excess funds to shareholders. Operating metrics have consistently improved since energy veteran Richard Kruger became CEO in early 2023. Quality assets, a low breakeven oil price, and a healthy balance sheet combine to offer downside protection. Suncor looks undervalued versus Canadian oilsands peers as well as big oils in America. I initiate coverage with a ‘buy’ rating based on a more reasonable EV/EBITDA ratio than the market currently reflects.

data.title: Rezolve: Expansion-Driven Growth Ahead, But Losses Deepen, Hold (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Introduction and Thesis Statement

Since my previous bullish article on Rezolve AI PLC (RZLV), the stock price has depreciated by more than 50% due to several reasons, one being the deepening net loss, which is currently trailing at -$217.53 million compared to the 2024 net loss of $172.65 million. In the first half of the year, the net loss hit $57.852 million compared to a net loss of $12.967 million in the first half of 2024, representing a 346.0% YoY increase in net loss.

In my previous article, I stressed the possible financial breakthrough in this company, and I still believe it is on the right trajectory despite the current loss. While I initially held a buy rating, in this article, I am downgrading Rezolve AI PLC to a hold driven by global expansion, which remains an optimistic outlook. However, due to the high operating costs involved in scaling up, I believe it might take time to turn around profitability. As such, the current stock volatility is likely to persist in the short term.

As my previous bullish article suggests, the bullish move held for a short while before recording a strong bearish reversal, which is also seen in the price return against the S&P 500. I believe this bearish move stems from high expenditure in expansion commitments, which has widened the price return gap with the S&P 500 price return of 72.78% outperforming Rezolve’s price return at -69.45%.

Following the partnerships discussed in my previous article, the company is executing these partnerships through intensive expansions. I will unearth why, despite the bearish move, there is still a bright future.

Expansion Benefits Seem Imminent

I believe that despite the increasing expenditure on expansion, with its operating expenses surging from $26.1 million in 2023 to the trailing $165.6 million, which pushes the stock price and earnings down, I view this as the price Rezolve must pay to capture a larger share of the rapidly growing Gen AI solutions market. I notice that last year (2024), the Gen AI solutions market volume reached $70-$140 billion. This market volume is highly driven by Business-to-Business (B2B) Gen AI solutions, which are estimated to reach a market volume of around $480 to $450 billion by 2028 at a CAGR of between 47% and 50%.

Now, given this growth in Gen AI, as an investor, my question is what the company is doing to maximize its reach in capturing this market. My finding is that following the partnerships I discussed in my previous article, the company executed through expansion. For example, the company has so far appointed Elizabeth Lachhar, a former Microsoft executive, as Senior Vice President of U.S Sales. Lachhar will oversee the go-to-market and drive enterprise adoption of the Rezolve Brain Commerce platform in the U.S. In the U.S, AI solutions are gaining traction from businesses that are looking forward to enhancing their shoppers. During this holiday season, around 86% of businesses cite that AI search will help them reach a larger market, which is likely to boost sales. In my view, coming from a tech giant like Microsoft, she brings a wealth of experience and expertise having worked in the technology sector for more than 20 years. In her tenure at Microsoft, she oversaw U.S enterprise sales accounts for more than $3 billion annually, among other achievements, which makes it a smart appointment in this company.

Given this optimistic view of the external market, Rezolve is investing a substantial amount in the new structure to reach this market, enabling sustained global expansion, profitability, and scaled execution. Beyond the new structure of positions, the company plans to add approximately 40 sales representatives in the U.S and 20-30 across Europe to establish a solid reach for agentic commerce. In Europe, around 33% of businesses are expected to incorporate agentic AI by 2028, up from the current 1%.

To this point, it is now clear that external tailwinds are the major driver in Rezolve’s investment in expansion. For this reason, I believe the company must achieve this at a cost, and it explains the increased costs such as selling, general & admin from $137.72 million in 2024 to the trailing $163.96 million.

Following this expansion, I believe that in 2026, the company is likely to start recording a sustainable revenue growth trend. This is because when I look at the planned enterprise AI event on January 28, 2026, Rezolve will demonstrate how its AI agents can autonomously resolve around 70% of the employee support tickets. Interestingly, this investment in expansion expands the company’s revenue base, which I will explore in more detail below.

Financials and Valuation

Even though the bottom line is poorly performing, resulting in the current bearish move, the top-line continues to spark a sustained growth ahead, looking at the trailing 307.81% growth YoY from 29.46% in 2024. This indicates that the outlook of my previous article, which focused on partnership sparking a revenue breakthrough, is showing a positive outcome. Consensus revenue estimate for FY2025 also reflects this revenue growth optimism at $40.44 million, representing 21,437.18% growth YoY. With the optimism expected in 2026, this revenue is projected to double, reaching $98.50 million and, even better, $203 million by 2027.

This might seem like an overambitious outlook, but based on the expansion into high-return regions, such as the U.S and Europe, the company has initiated a revenue guidance of $500 million Annual Recurring Revenue [ARR]. This is after securing $90 million in ARR YTD and now aiming to raise it to $150 million for 2025. This suggests that a solid demand momentum is likely to be experienced in 2026, given the visible customer pipeline. So far, the company has worked with over 100 enterprises globally, including Rakuten Group, New Era, and professional services such as Tata Consulting Services and PwC. A company like PwC is investing $1 billion in AI, which enables Rezolve to generate revenue by offering its services.

Moving to profitability, where the aspect of holding comes into place. Due to additional costs associated with expansion efforts, as seen in the operating expense for selling and administration previously mentioned, the net loss in 2025 has deepened to $217.53 million from $172.65 million in 2024.

This is also reflected in the consensus estimated EPS for FY2025, which is estimated to hit -$0.22. However, with the optimism expected from the realized value of intensive expenditure in 2025, the EPS might reach -$0.06, which represents a significant improvement of around 75.28%. Therefore, the current bearish sentiment, as suggested by the fundamentals above, indicates that this is a short-term hiccup that is likely to give way to a bullish reversal when projected expansion revenues begin to materialize and expenses stabilize.

I will now proceed to valuation using the EV/EBITDA multiple, as this company is at the execution stage of expansion, which is expected to incur high costs. I will compare its EV/EBITDA ratio to that of its peers, HIVE Digital Technologies Ltd. (HIVE) and Porch Group, Inc. (PRCH). The EV/EBITDA ratio of Rezolve is -5.92x, which is the lowest among its peers, compared to HIVE at 7.35x and PRCH at 20.49x. At deepening losses with Rezolve, it suggests that the company is experiencing short-term margin compression. Increased spending on operational costs, deepening losses, is pushing this seemingly low valuation. However, based on the fundamentals above regarding expansion and the expected returns, such as $500 ARR, it is also a reason why I am rating it a hold, as I believe this is a short-term hiccup in preparation for long-term and grounded profitability. The future of a bullish reversal hinges on the success of this expansion investment, which is why investors should keep an eye on margin recovery as a sign of a bullish reversal.

Investment Risks

Expansion Outpacing Profitability

After Rezolve H1 2025 reported a revenue $6.3 million, representing 426% growth YoY, it recorded an adjusted EBITDA loss of $17.7 million, which is a slight improvement from the estimated loss of $18.7 million. This suggests that the top-line growth remains explosive while operating losses remain high. Since the company plans to proceed with hiring in its expansion efforts, as previously stated, this risks consistent operating losses, which could continue to suppress EBITDA. This may result in sustained losses rather than the expected margin improvement, limiting the availability of capital for future expansion.

Margin Recovery Hinging on Execution

After Rezolve announced surpassing $50 billion in GMV earlier this year in April, with 16.5 million monthly active users and over 8 billion + API calls, margins remain compressed. The scale execution is showing a positive uptake. Still, the margin remains compressed, raising concerns about whether 2026 can achieve monetization as planned by turning GMV and user activity into sustainable EBITDA growth. The rise in operational costs is high compared to the monetization speed, raising concerns about whether margin recovery will be achieved at the same pace. This could leave Rezolve structurally weak compared to its peers, considering it is still at the lowest EV/EBITDA.

Conclusion

There is a sound reason to be optimistic with Rezolve given the above fundamentals. However, these fundamentals have not yet been reflected in realized value, with deepening losses driving the bearish move, which is why I am reiterating a Hold rating.

data.title: The Power Divide: China, U.S. And The Future Of The Grid
data.description:
Half the world’s electricity comes from two nations: China and the U.S. Their competing grids will define how the world powers the digital age.

Two Differing Blueprints for the Global Power Grid

Every second, half of the electricity running through the world’s grids originates from two countries: China and the U.S. China’s State Grid Corporation now transmits more electrons each day than all of Europe combined.

Scale, however, hides a deeper asymmetry. The U.S. still consumes roughly twice as much electricity per person as China. China’s grid powers production, steel, solar panels, batteries, and machinery exported to the world. America’s grid powers consumption, homes, data centers, transport, and comfort.

China and the U.S. Produce Half the World’s Electricity

Electricity Consumption Per Person in U.S. Twice as High as China’s

Energy-use structures underline that contrast. In 2023, industry absorbed nearly 60 percent of China’s final energy consumption, while in the U.S., commercial and residential accounted for more than 70 percent.

China Electrifies Production; the U.S. Electrifies Consumption

These differences are structural. China’s grid serves factories, an instrument of industrial growth. America’s powers lifestyles, energy treated as a market commodity. How a nation uses electricity reveals not just its economy, but its ambitions.

Philosophies of Power: China Monetizes Control, America Monetizes Volatility

Electricity does not just power economies. It reveals how they think. The way a nation builds, owns, and governs its grid is an X-ray of its political DNA. China’s grid is an instrument of the state; America’s is a marketplace.

From Beijing to the provinces, China plans and delivers electricity through a single vertical chain. In the U.S., a patchwork of markets and commissions argues and adapts.

In China, energy planning runs through the National Development and Reform Commission (NDRC) and the National Energy Administration (NEA) to provincial execution. The NDRC sets investment and pricing frameworks and approves key policies; the NEA oversees dispatch, supervises implementation, and manages the energy transition. The State Grid Corporation of China (SGCC) controls five northern and central regions covering more than 80 percent of the population. One of its key tasks is to build and operate ultra-high-voltage transmission lines that carry power from resource-rich western and northern areas to the major load centers in the east and south. The China Southern Power Grid (CSG) oversees the southern manufacturing belt, and the Inner Mongolia Power Group, a regional subsidiary of State Grid, manages the western Inner Mongolia network. Together they form the world’s largest coordinated electricity system.

Generation is dominated by six state-owned giants, China Energy Investment, State Power Investment, Huaneng, Datang, Huadian, and Three Gorges, which build and operate plants to meet national quotas. Overlap is deliberate: redundancy guarantees supply. The outcome is speed, scale, and predictability.

China’s Regulatory Framework

The U.S. operates through a decentralized web of regulators, markets, and utilities rather than a single national system. The Federal Energy Regulatory Commission (FERC) oversees interstate transmission and wholesale power markets, while fifty state commissions regulate retail prices and distribution utilities. Regional transmission organizations such as PJM, MISO, and CAISO coordinate generation and grid operations across multiple states but own no assets. ERCOT in Texas runs largely on its own, reflecting the state’s long-standing preference for competition and independence from federal oversight.

Ownership is equally diverse. Investor-owned utilities such as NextEra (NEE), Duke (DUK), and Southern Company (SO) coexist with public power agencies and rural cooperatives. Independent producers including Vistra (VST), Constellation (STZ), and NRG (NRG) compete in deregulated wholesale markets. This combination of private capital and public oversight encourages innovation and efficiency but often at the expense of coordination.

U.S.’s Regulatory Framework

In China, money flows through state banks to meet industrial and employment goals. In the U.S., it moves through markets, directed by investors seeking profit. China grows through coordination, while America advances through competition. One builds certainty; the other builds choice.

The Scale of Power in Two Growth Stories

Electricity generation tells a story of scale and direction. Since 2005, China’s power output has expanded nearly fivefold, growing at a compound annual rate of about 8 percent. The U.S., already a mature system, has grown by less than 1 percent a year. In 2005, America generated roughly twice as much electricity as China; today, the positions have reversed. China now produces more than twice as much power as the U.S.

China’s Capacity Expanded Nearly Fivefold While U.S. Growth Flat

The composition of that growth is equally revealing. China’s system has diversified without slowing. Coal remains the backbone, but new capacity in wind, solar, hydro, and nuclear has added more than six trillion kilowatt-hours since 2005. The result is a power system that is both immense and varied, built for industry, expanding through renewables, and guided by state design.

China: Coal Dominates, But Renewables and Nuclear Power Expand Rapidly

The U.S. system has evolved more than it has expanded. Natural gas replaced coal as the dominant source, while renewables, particularly wind and solar, grew rapidly from a low base yet total generation has barely increased. The American grid has become cleaner and more efficient, but not larger, a reflection of modest demand growth and rising efficiency rather than industrial expansion.

U.S.: Gas Replaces Coal, While Renewables Rise from a Small Base

The data highlight the core difference between the two models. China’s growth is physical, driven by construction and scale. America’s growth is compositional, driven by substitution and efficiency. One builds new capacity; the other optimizes the old.

When Electricity Pricing Reflects Policy

Chinese electricity costs about half the U.S. level and fluctuates far less. The difference is structural. China finances and regulates power as infrastructure; America prices it as a market commodity.

In China, the National Development and Reform Commission (NDRC) sets benchmark tariffs and allows prices to move within a ±20 percent band, giving limited flexibility while preserving stability for households and factories. When fuel costs rise, state utilities often absorb part of the increase rather than pass it to consumers. Centralized procurement and low-cost domestic engineering keep capital recovery per kilowatt hour modest. Prices vary by region but remain tightly managed: eastern coastal provinces, Hainan, Guangdong, Jiangsu, Zhejiang, Shanghai, and Beijing, typically record tariffs 20–30 percent above the national average, reflecting higher demand and denser load centers.

Market reforms are widening the space for competition but within state boundaries. In 2023, power-trading centers handled nearly 5,700 terawatt hours, equal to approximately 60 percent of national consumption, up from less than 17 percent in 2016. Most trading remains within provinces, though inter-provincial exchanges are expanding as Beijing builds toward a unified national power market by 2030. Even as trading grows, the state anchors prices. Financing, fuel, and infrastructure remain publicly controlled, and most generation is funded by low-cost state credit. The result is a guided market, competitive in allocation, not in price. Stability outweighs transparency, and affordability outranks return.

Across the Pacific, America follows the opposite creed. It prices electricity as a market, not a mandate. Every cost, fuel, finance, or failure, flows straight to the consumer. Volatility is the price of freedom. The Federal Energy Regulatory Commission (FERC) oversees interstate transmission and wholesale pricing, while fifty state commissions regulate retail tariffs and distribution. Power markets are coordinated by regional transmission organizations such as PJM, MISO, and CAISO, which balance generation across states but own no assets. ERCOT in Texas runs largely on its own, reflecting the state’s preference for independence and competition.

This fragmentation defines the U.S. grid. Eastern coastal states, Maine, New York, New Jersey, and Massachusetts, record regional averages about 40–60 percent above the national level, while California and Hawaii stand as extremes, with some of the highest electricity costs in the U.S. The U.S. system passes costs through instantly. When gas prices or capacity payments rise, utilities adjust tariffs through riders (automatic cost pass-through clauses) and surcharges. Market-based dispatch and private ownership encourage efficiency and innovation, but they also amplify price swings.

The Great Price Divide: China Anchors, America Accelerates

Residential

Commercial

Model Assumptions. Projections combine U.S. data from the EIA reference and side-case scenarios with the VanEck proprietary China Power Model, a continuously updated, data-driven framework that tests multiple growth trajectories rather than a single forecast. The model simulates the interaction of four structural drivers, fuel costs, electricity demand, renewable share, and carbon or regulatory costs, each calibrated through observed elasticities. The reference case assumes steady demand growth and balanced policy; the high and low cases bracket outcomes shaped by faster digitalization and efficiency gains or by weaker construction and slower industrial recovery. Over time, the model captures the system’s structural decoupling from fossil fuel prices as renewables expand and policy coordination deepens.

The two systems could not be more different. China regulates electricity to shield its economy from volatility; the U.S. prices it to expose its economy to efficiency. One builds certainty, the other choice. Both succeed on their own terms, one through control, the other through competition. Yet both now face the same frontier: as the grid becomes intelligent and demands more digital, the line between command and competition is beginning to blur.

The Age of Electric Sovereignty Is the Next Frontier

In the decade ahead, three forces—state, market, and cloud—will converge into an intelligent power economy, where algorithms, not administrators, balance the flow of energy across nations.

China will embed digital intelligence within its hierarchy, and the U.S. will unleash it through competition. Both are racing toward the same horizon: a self-governing grid that manages itself in real time.

The world’s largest technology firms are already building that future. Their data centers are no longer passive consumers of power but active governors of it, buying, storing, and trading electricity like capital. Energy is becoming liquidity, moving instantly across networks and borders.

The nation-state will endure, but it will now share the stage with digital empires that command both computation and current. China’s strength lies in abundance and control; America’s in innovation and capital. Yet both face a new kind of rival: the corporations that command the cloud.

Electricity is no longer infrastructure. It is digital intelligence, strategy, and sovereignty combined. The next superpower may not be a country at all, but whoever masters the current that powers both machines and minds.

IMPORTANT DISCLOSURES

Please note that VanEck may offer investments products that invest in the asset class(es) or industries included herein.

This is not an offer to buy or sell, or a recommendation to buy or sell any of the securities, financial instruments or digital assets mentioned herein. The information presented does not involve the rendering of personalized investment, financial, legal, tax advice, or any call to action. Certain statements contained herein may constitute projections, forecasts and other forward-looking statements, which do not reflect actual results, are for illustrative purposes only, are valid as of the date of this communication, and are subject to change without notice. Actual future performance of any assets or industries mentioned is unknown. Information provided by third party sources are believed to be reliable and have not been independently verified for accuracy or completeness and cannot be guaranteed. VanEck does not guarantee the accuracy of third-party data. The information herein represents the opinion of the author(s), but not necessarily those of VanEck or its other employees.

For other important disclosures please read more.

All investing is subject to risk, including the possible loss of the money you invest. As with any investment strategy, there is no guarantee that investment objectives will be met and investors may lose money. Diversification does not ensure a profit or protect against a loss in a declining market. Past performance is no guarantee of future results.
