data.title: A Second Anktiva Approval Could Be The Catalyst ImmunityBio Needs
data.description:
Thesis

ImmunityBio (IBRX) reported a 3Q25 GAAP EPS loss of just -$0.07, which beat estimates by about $0.04. Elsewhere, revenue came in at $32.06 million, up a whopping 425% year-over-year and slightly above expectations by $0.18 million. The company also managed to end the quarter with $257.8 million in cash and marketable securities, up quite a bit from $153.7 million in 2Q25. Overall, the main driver of their sales and the stock would be Anktiva sales. Sales for the treatment have actually shown quite good momentum since last year; it now makes up essentially all of ImmunityBio's revenue. The company has some plans to capitalise on this and is actively testing out quite a few combination therapies with Anktiva in other treatment areas, as I'll explain. In terms of the stock, things have been mostly flat over the past year, with some pretty high investor expectations for more Anktiva sales growth baked into the stock. I think we should still see more momentum in terms of Anktiva revenue, but the biggest win for the company will be getting a second approved Anktiva therapy on the market. This is the biggest mid-term catalyst I see for the stock.

3Q25 earnings

ImmunityBio's 3Q25 earnings show us a company right in the middle of commercial expansion, driven mainly by Anktiva uptake. Product revenue jumped to $31.8 million, a hefty 434% year-over-year increase, which indicates strong physician/payor adoption in BCG-unresponsive NMIBC with CIS. Year-to-date sales managed to hit $74.7 million, which is tied to a 467% jump in unit volume versus the same period in 2024. This, for me, is evidence that Anktiva is moving away from just an early launch treatment momentum to much broader clinical use.

The company also strengthened its balance sheet considerably. They managed to increase their cash position to $257.8 million despite continuing to invest heavily in R&D, which came in at $51.2 million. Operating losses also narrowed significantly, dropping from $80.3 million in Q3 2024 to $55.6 million in Q3 2025. This was mostly driven by higher revenue and reduced SG&A spending, even though R&D was still high to support some ongoing clinical programs. Management added that the high spending on R&D was also driven by higher manufacturing and distribution costs from expanded production. They also cited higher license fees.

The company reported about $234.6 million of net cash used in operating activities over the nine months that ended on September 30th, 2025. So dividing that nine-month total by 3 quarters gives us an average quarterly operating cash burn of about $78.2 million. So if we take their $257.8 million cash balance and an average quarterly operating burn of $78 million, I see ImmunityBio as having an estimated cash runway of about 3 to 4 quarters, or roughly 10 months, at current spending levels. However, like we're seeing, continued growth in Anktiva revenue could extend this runway significantly. Most importantly, continued clinical momentum boosted the financial results, which is what investors wanted to see. We also got some promising early data in glioblastoma, NSCLC, and lymphoma, which we will get to shortly. All around, it seems ImmunityBio has good potential in its BioShield platform. Since earnings shares have been on an upward trend over November, rising by over 13%.

Anktiva momentum

Anktiva is an IL-15 receptor agonist that was actually approved back in April 2024. It is used along with BCG to treat adults with BCG-unresponsive non-muscle invasive bladder cancer (NMIBC) with carcinoma in situ, with or without papillary tumours. The therapy works by essentially activating a whole host of cells in the patient's body, including NK cells, CD8+ killer T cells, and CD4+ helper T cells, while at the same time generating memory T-cell responses. This should strengthen the body's ability to eliminate the tumour cells and maintain long-term immune surveillance. Now, since its commercial launch in mid-2024, Anktiva has pretty much driven all of ImmunityBio's product revenue. We've seen sales rapidly expand from roughly $6 million in 3Q24 to about $31.8 million in 3Q25. The sales have totalled $74.7 million year-to-date in 2025 alone, which is about 467% unit growth.

Now, if we assume Anktiva keeps growing at its average recent pace of roughly 30% increase in revenue per quarter, sales would scale pretty rapidly as adoption deepens and treatment durations extend. At that same trajectory, quarterly revenue would rise from the $31.8 million we saw in 3Q25 to roughly $41 million in 4Q25, $53 million in 1Q26, and over $100 million per quarter by early 2027. It would push annual Anktiva sales above $400 million within two years. But that scenario would be a steady continuation of current commercial momentum without major market or payer disruptions or step-change expansions into new indications. The trouble is that it is still quite early days for Anktiva, and we're only now just getting a grip on the direction sales are heading in.

Elsewhere in the pipeline

ImmunityBio also gave some updates on some early clinical results beyond just Anktiva. As you would expect, Anktiva cannot drive sales forever, and the high R&D expense of $51.2 million this quarter shows that they are trying to expand into other high-need areas like brain cancer, lung cancer, and lymphoma. In just a small compassionate-use/early trial for recurrent glioblastoma (GBM), five patients who were treated with a combination of Anktiva, a natural-killer (NK) cell therapy, and the device Optune Gio achieved 100% disease control. On top of that, three patients showed a response, two of which were near-complete. The remaining two patients showed a stable disease. Most importantly, all had increased lymphocyte counts, which is what the company was hoping to see; it showed that there is actually an immune system activation. Based on that, ImmunityBio is set to initiate a formal, randomised registration trial in second-line GBM patients.

Moving on to non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC), the company managed to conduct a global Phase 3 trial (ResQ201A), which tested Anktiva combined with a checkpoint inhibitor via tislelizumab/TEVIMBRA/BeOne with the added combination of standard chemotherapy (docetaxel). This was compared to just chemo alone in patients where the tumours are resistant to prior checkpoint inhibitor therapy. The trial actually built on much earlier-phase data, which previously showed Anktiva's ability to reverse lymphopenia (low NK and T cell counts) and also restore immune responsiveness in the NSCLC patients. It led to a quite meaningful median overall survival benefit compared with historical controls. Essentially, that means the patients in the company's study actually lived longer, on median, than what has usually been seen in past studies.

Biggest risks

The biggest red flag investors will notice is that ImmunityBio has a significant debt load of about $861 million. That is with about $257.8 million in cash. So it leaves a net debt of roughly $603 million. So while the cash provides some liquidity, they need that continued Anktiva revenue to keep supporting operations and R&D. Based on the latest filings, a big portion of their total debt is actually from related-party debt. Therefore, about $500.8 million from related-part and about $360 million in other debt/notes. Elsewhere, what will strike investors is that most of the company's future growth depends heavily on successful trials beyond just Anktiva. We have NSCLC, glioblastoma, and lymphoma programs all in the works. Therefore, with already a lot of expectation baked in the stock, if there are any failures/delays in readouts, it could materially impact the stock. It makes ImmunityBio shares carry a risk for investors.

Valuation

ImmunityBio currently trades at about $2.36 per share. Over the past 3 months, the stock has traded pretty flat, up just 0.85%. They hold a market cap of $2.32 billion and an enterprise value of $2.93 billion. Despite the strong revenue we're seeing from Anktiva, traditional valuation metrics like EV to sales still screen as pretty elevated at 24.5x on a trailing twelve-month basis. It's a sign of the market's expectation for more of the same high sales growth. They hold a pretty hefty cash of $257.8 million versus total debt of $861 million, so the balance sheet provides some liquidity to support their ongoing clinical programs. So the high EV to sales shows us the premium investors are placing on ImmunityBio's pipeline potential rather than current profitability.

Looking ahead

Investors would want to keep a close eye on that Anktiva revenue figure. We don't want it to slow down anytime soon since the company is still undergoing a hefty R&D spend with those combination therapies progressing down the pipeline. The next big data readout I see is the ResQ201A Phase 3 trial in checkpoint‑resistant NSCLC. Early-phase data already suggest that Anktiva can reverse lymphopenia somewhat. So it makes this upcoming readout pretty critical for confirming efficacy. A secondary upcoming readout from the Phase 2 GBM trial (QUILT‑3.078) is also something to look out for. Overall, I'm quite bullish on ImmuniBio stock, despite the lack of positive stock movement we've seen so far.

data.title: Visa: Payment Volumes Facing Recession Fears
data.description:
Visa Inc. (V) is one of the biggest card payment networks in the world, alongside its close peer Mastercard Incorporated (MA). It essentially provides the infrastructure for card issuers/banks to connect with one another when the cardholder/consumer essentially provides a payment towards the merchant/payment receiver. Visa has a wide moat as the barriers to entry are extremely high within the payment network, as Visa alongside Mastercard essentially runs a duopoly for the top payment networks globally. The only other network that can compete is American Express Company (AXP), differentiated by providing the card, essentially acting as the issuer and the network.

Visa is way more concentrated in the US compared to Mastercard when it comes to payment volume, so volatility and weakness in the US economy would essentially affect Visa harder than Mastercard. With more and more US states at or on the brink of a recession, Visa will continue to be impacted with potentially less spending and worse consumer sentiment. If inflation rates continue to remain at elevated levels, Visa will benefit from higher prices as it receives a percentage and usually a fixed amount of every transaction. Payment networks are also under threat from the rise of digital currencies and tokenization, and though Visa will be a major player in it, it wouldn't be able to have a similar moat as it does in the payment network market. With mixed valuations, I'm rating Visa at a 'Hold' as there are more challenges ahead for Visa rather than catalysts.

Payment Volumes

Visa beat top and bottom line in Q4 FY25, with revenue growing 12% in Q4, with payment volumes growing 9% YoY. Most companies especially since we are in the AI boom, tend to trade in quarters, but Visa is slightly different as volatility is lower since the results tend to be more predictable. Everyone needs to spend money, so Visa's revenue won't fluctuate or have huge surprises, being far more exposed to macroeconomic conditions like unemployment and inflation since it directly influences spending.

Revenue and margins are obviously important, but I believe the main focus point for Visa is always taking a look at payment volumes and comparing them to its close peers, especially when the economy is performing poorly and credit card debt continues to rise. This is seen clearly today with Moody's chief economist saying that half of the US states are or at the brink of a recession, which should be slightly concerning. If a change in trend does happen due to already existing poor macroeconomic environment, Visa will get hit the hardest since most of their purchase volume is concentrated in the US, way less diversified than Mastercard. Until the debt problem gets fixed or unemployment/job openings improve, there are currently more obstacles for Visa than catalysts.

As seen above, Visa's purchase volume is way larger than Mastercard's in the US, as Visa is more concentrated in the US compared to Mastercard.

Splitting Visa's nominal payment volumes and net revenue by US and International (excluding cash volume)

Nominal Payment Volume 2023 2024 2025
US 50% 49.2% 48.9%
International 50% 50.8% 51.1%
Net Revenue
US 43.3% 41.1% 39.1%
International 56.7% 58.9% 60.9%

Mastercard's exposure to the US is clearly far less than Visa's, being only at 29.8% of gross dollar volume (GDV) in the most recent quarter, whilst Visa's nominal payment volume for the US stood at 48.9%.

It appears that Visa's exposure to the US is slowly reducing over the years, but it is honestly more due to the fact that international spending is growing faster than in the US. As seen above, Mastercard's GDV for the US grew only 7% whilst the rest of the world grew 10%. Using values rather than percentages, US GDV grew $51B for Mastercard, whilst the rest of the world grew $193B YoY.

Growth in payment volume by the rest of the world is faster than in the US. It isn't bad, but it is more of an indicator that Mastercard will likely gain more than Visa since it has a higher exposure in the US. Seen in the 2nd table as well, Visa's international revenue has been growing far faster than US revenue for 2 years now, growing mid-double digits 2 years in a row, whilst the US only grew mid-single digits.

This results in higher growth rates for Mastercard, continuing to grow mid-double digits, whilst 4 quarters in a row now compared to Visa's low-double digit growth/single-digit growth in recent times.

Threats to Payment Volume Growth, And A Potential Catalyst

On top of the mentioned, more and more US states are falling into a recession, there is also negative sentiment surrounding spending, which could fall into a downward spiral, and credit card debt and delinquencies are hitting highs as spending from the general public may fall. This is all whilst higher-spending cardholders would continue to drive more growth, as AXP raised the lower end of their guidance in the most recent quarter, despite their huge exposure to the US (65% network volume in the US).

Visa's payment volume hasn't been affected yet, as growth rates for both debit and credit haven't had a huge change in growth compared to the prior year, but investors should remain cautious with the worsening macroeconomic conditions. Any increase in regulation will influence Visa if spending in the US is limited.

Credit card debt continues to rise, with $1.23T in credit card balances in Q3 up $24B from Q2, whilst the delinquency rate is at higher rates on a 10Y scale. Job openings have also been trending down recently, whilst unemployment has also started to tick up. With more and more US states falling into a recession as macroeconomic conditions continue to worsen, this only leads to lower consumer spending since individual lower-income households will feel more pressure, which will impact payment volumes and Visa's revenue.

Monitoring credit card behaviour as well as buy now pay later schemes is very important, as when disposable income gets constrained, one of the main ways households would get by would be credit or paying in installments. PayPal's buy now pay later volume and active accounts grew 20% YoY, whilst Affirm grew 52% YoY in total transactions with a 96% repeat transaction rate. This should be slightly worrying as continuous credit spending, as well as delinquencies (though it has been trending down but remains at elevated levels) will affect payment volume if some form of regulation comes in to control the irrational spending. As these balances continue to hit all-time highs, intervention might not be far away if delinquencies start rising.

From the earnings call:

In Q4, with higher spending cardholders driving more of the growth, that's consistent with what we see across the U.S. economy. And so that all gives us good reason over that data to say that the consumer has remained resilient. That is our -- that is what we saw in FY '25, and that is our assumption going into FY '26.

It was also mentioned that higher spending cardholders are driving more of the growth, which should raise some concerns for shareholders, as lower spending cardholders continue to be impacted today, as they also continue to believe these conditions will continue into FY26, since they expect the same situation where higher spending cardholders will drive the growth.

Due to this scenario, the probability of interest rate cuts essentially suggests that the Fed is going to continue to cut rates, assuming that lower rates mean more job openings from higher borrowing, lower unemployment and more spending (less savings). Lower interest rates also mean potential credit card APRs might fall, which could reduce credit card balances since individuals would have to pay less interest.

However, the effects of the interest rates in recent times haven't really been felt, so despite seeming like a catalyst with interest rates continuing to fall, this hasn't really reflected in the economy yet, as job openings are still low, with credit card debt continuing to rise. This should also be a cautious sign for investors, but continuous interest rate cuts could benefit Visa, though the effects haven't been felt in the short term. With a new Fed chair coming up picked by President Trump, we could see even more dovish sentiment which aligns with Trump's plans for wanting faster interest rate cuts.

If this scenario does happen, where interest rates continue to get cut, job openings rise with unemployment falling, credit card balance falls, US states crawl out of a recession and Visa's growth is not reliant on high spending cardholders, inflation would probably rise, which is beneficial to Visa. Higher inflation simply means higher prices, which means higher payment volumes which will benefit Visa. With more interest rate cuts expected in 2026 with the new Fed chair, which will align with President Trump's desire for lower interest rates (since Trump is literally picking this person), Visa's revenue growth could be accelerated with better macroeconomic conditions. Until there are better economic conditions realised by the numerous statistics mentioned, Visa's payment volumes will likely continue to decelerate as more and more US states fall into a recession.

The other huge concern is that there is a shift from card payments towards crypto payments, as crypto continues to be one of the hottest topics in the market. Mentioned in my Mastercard article, a survey from Deloitte with North American CFOs found that only 1% didn't envision using crypto for business functions in the long term. It is not that Visa won't play a massive part in the rise of these digital currencies; they will play a role, but the competition will be way larger than what they have right now in the card payment networks. So if there is a noticeable shift away from card payments, Visa will take a hit since enterprises have many other options when it comes to these transactions. With the digital currencies' popularity starting in the US with Trump deregulating the market more, like signing the GENIUS Act to lead the global digital currency revolution, Visa will also take a larger hit since it has more exposure in the US compared to Mastercard, which is more diversified internationally.

Valuations

Visa shouldn't be judged based on the sector median, since the market is hugely dominated by Visa and Mastercard with little competition due to their wide moat of huge barriers to entry. Since a new payment network would have to convince merchants globally to adopt their network, as well as convince issuers to use their network, and for consumers to have this new card, the process is near impossible for a new firm to even start making money.

Compared to Visa's 5Y average, Visa is trading at a historical discount on a P/E and a P/S basis, which could seem like an opportunity if Visa can adapt to its challenges, but trading lower than its historical averages shouldn't be a huge surprise since the challenges are now stacking up as growth rates may start to decelerate. If Visa adapts well, its current premiums may seem like an opportunity for a solid entry point.

Visa and Mastercard continue to trade at similar premiums on both P/E and P/S, with a justified larger premium to AXP, which is one of the only firms with similarities to Visa and Mastercard. With Visa trading at a lower P/E and Mastercard trading at a lower P/S just slightly, with the similarities of both Visa and Mastercard, there isn't a clear valuation difference between them as the trading patterns are heavily similar for both Visa and Mastercard. For investors wanting to start a position in Visa, its current valuations, especially below historical averages may be very attractive, considering Visa's revenue growth is rather 'guaranteed' since everyone needs to spend money as inflation would essentially lead to revenue growth since payment volumes would rise. For now, there is no disparity or opportunity with Visa's and Mastercard's valuations.

Final Thoughts

As AI valuations get higher and higher, Visa as a defensive stock in the portfolio isn't entirely a bad idea since it would be way less volatile than AI names, so whilst diversifying Visa should be one of the top choices. However, it is also facing challenges from one of the hottest topics in the markets right now, the rise of digital currencies and crypto whilst moving away from regular payments could seriously harm Visa as the strength of the moat, even if they are successful will be nowhere as wide as the one they have in the card payment network.

Investors need to continue to monitor macroeconomic conditions, as worsening conditions will simply create negative sentiment for Visa since it would affect revenue growth. However, until there is a clear trend of falling payment volumes, which hasn't shown in the most recent quarter, Visa's growth hasn't been impacted heavily just yet.

data.title: AGNC Vs. Annaly: Why I Am Buying Both For A 13% Average Yield
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Many investors who are focused on high dividend yields are interested in selecting assets from the mREITs sector. In my opinion, AGNC Investment Corp. (AGNC) and Annaly Capital Management (NLY) are the clear leaders in this selection. These firms are characterized by proven profitability and reliability, with solid performance and robust portfolios of mortgage-backed securities. Even though AGNC and NLY are assets that compete directly with each other, I believe that both can be included in your investment portfolio. My fundamental analysis has identified a favorable current market environment that supports the objectives of generating high passive returns from investments in the mREIT sector.

I suggest conducting a comparative analysis of these companies to identify their fundamental differences in investment strategy, portfolio management, and risk management approaches. Additionally, this article will provide a detailed assessment of the competitive environment, where there are other alternative assets besides AGNC and NLY, including Dynex Capital (DX). It is now, during a period of change in the Fed's monetary policy, the combination of NLY's diversified model and AGNC's "pure" agency strategy that allows long-term investors to build an optimal and profitable portfolio while minimizing potential risks.

The Structure of the Mortgage REIT Market

Identifying the main players in the mortgage REIT sector, it is worth paying attention to two key assets: AGNC Investment and Annaly Capital Management. These are the industry leaders in the mortgage REIT market, providing investors with similar financial products. As such, they're often seen as complementary assets in an income-focused investment portfolio.

Beyond these, there are other companies that could be competitors in attracting investor capital. Firstly, there are direct analogues in the form of agency mREITs. These firms have a similar business model focused on generating income from the spread between rates using leverage, but with smaller AUM. This includes such well-known assets as ARMOUR Residential REIT (ARR) (its advantage is high dividends (16.44%), but their securities are historically volatile) and Orchid Island Capital (ORC) (a smaller fund (AUM $1.21 billion) with very high returns but involving higher investment risks).

Separately, I would like to highlight Dynex Capital, the most reliable among mREITs, which is characterized by its ability to operate in unstable market conditions. In a recent article where I recommended buying DX, I mentioned the potential positive impact of lower interest rates on increasing the spread between portfolio yields and borrowing costs. As a result, DX investors have the opportunity to find themselves in a winning position by investing in the most reliable mREIT securities.

AGNC and NLY also face competition from non-agency mREITs, which, although they create indirect competition, also have a track record of working with residential mortgage securities. These assets are, in my opinion, riskier, as they take on additional credit risk associated with mortgages that are not guaranteed by the government. They also have mortgage servicing rights (MSRs) in their portfolio structure. The major players in non-agency mREITs include Rithm Capital (RITM) (their portfolio is highly diversified, allowing them to be less sensitive to interest rates but more dependent on the economic conditions of the US economy). Also included here are Two Harbors Investment Corp. (TWO) (their main focus is on MSR, which has provided additional protection during the period of Fed interest rate hikes) and Chimera Investment (CIM) (their portfolio is particularly focused on high-risk mortgage loans, which makes me reluctant to consider them a reliable investment).

Also, in the battle for investor capital, AGNC and NLY face competition from various commercial mREITs. Their business model is characterized by providing loans to businesses that use commercial real estate as collateral. They have a high dividend yield, but they operate in a completely different way. Notable commercial mREITs include Starwood Property Trust (STWD), Blackstone Mortgage Trust (BXMT), and Arbor Realty Trust (ABR). I see no point in dwelling on them in detail, as these assets are of no interest to me in the long term.

If looking for the asset most similar to AGNC and NLY, it would still be DX. One thing I appreciate about them is that most of their portfolio (93% of the total share, or about $14.7 billion) is invested in agency mortgage-backed securities. The other 7% of the portfolio is made up of CMBS/CMBS assets (about $1.1 billion). That creates a super reliable mortgage securities portfolio, which maximizes the company's reliability.

Fundamental Similarities Between AGNC and NLY and Their Main Differences

As you have already understood, AGNC and NLY are the most popular players in the mortgage REIT sector. This is why many investors consider them interchangeable, though there are important differences between their strategies. In defining their similarities, it's worth noting that they have a similar business model. Both make money exclusively on interest rate spreads. What happens is that they raise capital in the short-term credit market at lower rates in order to further invest in long-term mortgage securities, which have higher yields. Both companies use leverage to maximize their income. That means they raise borrowed capital in amounts that exceed their own funds.

The primary asset that AGNC and NLY focus on is agency MBS. The primary issuers of these securities are Fannie Mae, Freddie Mac, and Ginnie Mae. The significance of these mortgage-backed securities lies in the fact that they carry no credit risk. This is due to the fact that the borrower's insolvency is guaranteed by a government agency (which issues these agency MBS). For instance, if a borrower is unable to continue making mortgage payments, then the government does so. Because of this, the risk of default for such assets is essentially non-existent. The AGNC and NLY portfolios contain the most reliable type of securities related to the real estate market and mortgage lending in the US.

The similar quality between AGNC and NLY is that the book value of their asset portfolios is highly dependent on the actions of the Federal Reserve. Specifically, changes in interest rates lead to potential changes in the value of the companies themselves. The most damaging scenario is a rise in rates, as this leads to a decline in book value. Meanwhile, the inversion of the yield curve itself leads to a reduction in profits. This is the situation where short-term rates begin to rise faster than long-term rates, which makes them higher.

But a deeper look at the fundamental similarities between AGNC and NLY reveals four main differences. They are their portfolio structure, differences in leverage, differences in dividend policy, and different valuations.

Criterion #1: The portfolio structure between these companies varies due to their different strategies

The AGNC focuses on positioning itself exclusively as an entity that works only with agency mortgage-backed securities. Therefore, the composition of their portfolio consists almost entirely of agency MBS secured by government obligations. This strategy's advantage is that there's no credit risk. However, at the same time, they have a higher sensitivity to interest rates. The NLYs have a more diversified portfolio structure because, besides agency MBS, they have securities without government guarantees and mortgage servicing rights. Earlier, NLY had another type of asset in its portfolio structure related to commercial real estate. However, they sold it, concentrating on residential real estate.

Criterion #2: Differences in leverage

Compared to NLY, AGNC has higher leverage (~7.2x - 7.5x) versus (~5.7x - 6.0x). On one hand, it might seem like this makes AGNC's investment strategy riskier. But this is not the case at all. Let me remind you that their portfolio consists of the safest and most liquid assets on the mortgage market—agency MBS. As there is no default risk on these, AGNC's management is able to borrow more than NLY in order to maximize returns. As NLY also invests in risky assets, including MSR, they cannot afford the same leverage as AGNC.

Criterion #3: Differences in dividend policy

Here, it's very simple. While AGNC pays dividends to investors every month, NLY pays them quarterly. This difference is not important to me. But if you are an investor who lives on passive income from dividend payments, NLY is obviously less suitable for you. That said, AGNC has a higher forward dividend yield—13.73% versus 12.28% for NLY. The difference of almost 1.5% can be significant for the effectiveness of an income-oriented strategy.

Criterion #4: Different valuation (price to book value)

AGNC is a company that often has a premium in its valuation relative to its book value. The investors are keen to invest in this asset because it is safer and more predictable, is less volatile, and pays monthly dividends. The NLY has historically traded closer to its book value, and sometimes at a slight discount. Because of the structure of their asset portfolio and higher credit risk, however, they have phases of market conditions that cause investors to be more cautious.

Comparative Table of AGNC and NLY Fund Characteristics

The following is a brief comparative table of key criteria that allows you to determine the difference between AGNC and NLY.

Risks of the Investment Thesis

Determining the risks of recommending AGNC and NLY, it should be noted that the main threat is a sharp change in the Fed's monetary policy vector. For instance, it is possible due to the relevance of macroeconomic risks. Their influence could lead to a potential narrowing of spreads, which would be accompanied by rate volatility. Should inflation in the US begin to accelerate again (e.g., as a consequence of the long-term impact of tariff policy), the Federal Reserve may postpone decisions to lower rates. Because of this, the cost of raising capital for AGNC and NLY will remain high, further squeezing their margins. Ultimately, the companies' portfolio yields will decline, which could lead not so much to a reduction in dividend payments as to a decline in their market value. Because of this, the overall total return result will deteriorate, thus only passive dividend income will remain.

Conclusion

So, after looking at companies like AGNC Investment and Annaly Capital Management, I find them to be really attractive for investing. I recommend buying them. At the same time, I think these two assets complement each other and have the potential to keep giving good returns even when interest rates go down. Besides high dividend payments, these companies' shares have shown significant growth in market value over the past year. Therefore, their total return for the year is 27.22% and 31.67%, respectively.

When choosing between the two assets, AGNC is more suitable for investors who value monthly passive income. Their portfolio is more stable and secure due to the absence of default risk. If you're an investor who doesn't really care about getting monthly payments and wants an asset with a more balanced, diversified portfolio, then NLY is the better choice. Besides AGNC and NLY, my recommendations for buying into an income-focused portfolio include DX securities. These are the three most successful players in the mREITs market, in my opinion.
